title: When Few-Shot Stops Helping
concept: Diminishing returns and context-cost tradeoffs as example count grows; fine-tuning as the eventual alternative.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we discussed example ordering. What is 'recency bias' in a few-shot prompt?

options.text: The model places more weight on the examples closest to the end of the prompt.
The model only remembers the very first example it sees.
The model ignores examples that contain uppercase letters.

exercises.explain: Recency bias is the tendency of LLMs to be disproportionately influenced by the examples placed closest to the end of the prompt.

cards.title: More is Not Always Better
cards.body: If three examples make your LLM perform well, wouldn't thirty examples make it perfect? Surprisingly, no! More examples can actually confuse the model, slow it down, and drain your budget.

cards.title: Diminishing Returns & Fine-Tuning
cards.body: Diminishing returns in prompting means that adding more examples eventually stops improving accuracy, while constantly increasing your token costs and slowing down response times. At a certain point, transition to fine-tuning to permanently teach the model these patterns.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Desk
cards.body: Think of few-shot examples like sticky notes on your monitor. A few notes help you remember tasks, but plastering fifty notes covers your screen, costs too much to read, and makes you lose track of the most important instructions.

cards.title: The Cost of Extra Examples
cards.body: Going from 0 to 3 examples might boost your classifier accuracy from 60% to 85%. However, adding 20 more examples might only boost it to 86%, while quadrupling your API bill and making the model slower.

cards.title: The Long-Prompt Trap
cards.body: Many developers believe that larger context windows mean they should pack hundreds of examples into a prompt. In reality, models often suffer from 'lost in the middle' syndrome, ignoring instructions and examples buried in long prompts.

cards.title: Prompt Space vs. Model Weights
cards.body: Compare how few-shot prompting handles knowledge in the prompt versus how fine-tuning bakes it directly into the model structure.

left.text: Examples are loaded into active memory (context window) every single request, costing time and money.

right.text: Examples are baked directly into the model's weights during training, keeping prompts short and fast.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary trade-off of adding dozens of examples to a few-shot prompt?

options.text: It makes the model run much faster.
It increases token usage, leading to higher API costs and slower response times.
It bypasses the need for system instructions.

exercises.explain: Each extra example increases the prompt token count. Since LLM providers charge per token, longer prompts directly translate to higher operational costs and higher latency.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LLM performance scales up linearly forever as you add more few-shot examples.
exercises.explain: More examples eventually lead to flatlining performance (diminishing returns) while continually increasing costs and latency.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to identify the permanent training alternative to long few-shot prompts.
exercises.sentence: When few-shot prompting becomes too expensive, fine-tuning is the best alternative to embed patterns directly into the model.
exercises.explain: Fine-tuning is the process of training a model on a dataset to bake the patterns into its weights, removing the need for a long few-shot prompt.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks describes preparing a model for fine-tuning rather than few-shot prompting?

options.text: Let's add five more customer support examples to the system prompt to see if accuracy improves.
We will format 5,000 JSON lines of input-output pairs to run a gradient descent training job on the base model weights.
I will shuffle the order of these three examples to make sure the model doesn't favor the last one.

exercises.explain: Option B shows a typical automated dataset formatting process used to prepare thousands of examples for fine-tuning, rather than writing a prompt template.

exercises.prompt: You have built a few-shot prompt with 30 detailed examples. It is becoming very expensive, slow, and the LLM is starting to ignore the instructions at the beginning. What is the best engineering path forward?

options.text: Keep adding examples indefinitely because modern LLM context windows are infinite and free.
Switch to a smaller model and write longer explanations instead of using examples.
Transition to fine-tuning to bake the patterns into the model's weights, saving context space and cost.
Use random ordering of 100 examples to bypass the context window limits.

exercises.explain: Adding more examples eventually fills up the context window, increases costs, and degrades performance. When few-shot prompting hits this limit, fine-tuning is the correct alternative because it bakes the patterns directly into the model's weights without using up prompt space.
